GCE A LEVEL MARKING SCHEME


PREDICTED PAPER – MODERATE


A LEVEL
ECONOMICS – COMPONENT 2
A520U20-1


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.             PJJS*(S24-A520U20-1)
                                                2


About this marking scheme
The purpose of this marking scheme is to provide teachers, learners and other interested parties
with an understanding of the assessment criteria used to assess this specific assessment.

This predicted (Moderate) marking scheme mirrors the structure, mark allocations and assessment
objective weightings of the 2024 WJEC/Eduqas A Level Economics Component 2 paper. It is
pitched at a moderate level of difficulty so as to support learners preparing for the full range of
Component 2 questions. It is not an official WJEC document.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                            (A520U20-1)
                                                 3


                           GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE
Positive Marking
It should be remembered that learners are writing under examination conditions and credit should
be given for what the learner writes, rather than adopting the approach of penalising him/her for
any omissions. It should be possible for a very good response to achieve full marks and a very
poor one to achieve zero marks.

For each question there is a list of indicative content which suggests the range of economic
concepts, theory, issues and arguments which might be included in learners' answers. This is not
intended to be exhaustive and learners do not have to include all the indicative content to reach the
highest level of the mark scheme.

The level-based mark schemes sub-divide the total mark to allocate to individual assessment
objectives. Learners' responses to questions are assessed against the relevant individual
assessment objectives and they may achieve different bands within a single question.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                             (A520U20-1)

                          GCE A LEVEL ECONOMICS – COMPONENT 2
                           PREDICTED (MODERATE) MARK SCHEME

Question 1

                   Using the data, explain why house prices have risen faster than
   1      1                                                                                              [5]
                   earnings in the UK since 2000 (Figure 1).
  Band                     AO1                           AO2                             AO3

                          1 mark                     2 marks                          2 marks
                        Knowledge                 Good application                  Good analysis
    2      Clear knowledge of the              Strong use of Figures 1      Developed chain of reasoning
           determinants of house prices        and/or 2 to support the      explaining why prices have
           (demand/supply) is demonstrated.    argument.                    outpaced earnings.

                          1 mark                       1 mark                           1 mark
                    Limited knowledge            Limited application               Limited analysis
    1      Some awareness of price             Use of the data is limited   Chain of reasoning is
           determination is shown.             with few direct              unconvincing or lacking in
                                               references.                  clarity.

                         0 marks                        0 marks                        0 marks
    0      No knowledge or understanding       No valid application.        No valid analysis.
           shown.


Indicative content

AO1
House prices are set by the interaction of demand and supply. Shifts in either change the
equilibrium price. Persistent outward shifts in demand combined with an inelastic supply produce a
rising price trend.

AO2
Figure 1 shows that the UK house-price-to-earnings ratio has roughly doubled from around 4.2 in
2000 to around 8.3 in 2023, indicating that prices have grown almost twice as fast as earnings.
Figure 2 shows that housing completions have consistently fallen short of the 300,000 target –
confirming that supply has been slow to respond to demand.

AO3
Demand has risen faster than supply. A growing population, smaller household sizes, migration
and rising life expectancy have all increased the number of households seeking homes.
Historically low interest rates between 2009 and 2022 made mortgage borrowing cheaper,
supporting higher house prices. Much of the price-to-earnings rise occurred during this cheap-
credit era.
Government demand-side schemes (Help to Buy, Stamp Duty relief) further supported demand.
Supply is highly inelastic: planning restrictions, limited land release, skills shortages in construction
and the dominance of a few volume house-builders have prevented supply from responding.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                               (A520U20-1)
                                              5


Real wages stagnated between 2008 and 2015, so earnings did not keep pace with house prices
even when prices paused.
Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                          (A520U20-1)
                                                     6


                   Using the data, explain why the UK Government might want to
   1      2                                                                                            [5]
                   increase the supply of housing in the UK.
  Band                     AO2                                              AO3

                                                                          3 marks
                                                                      Excellent analysis
                                                 Well-developed chain of reasoning covering multiple
                                                 reasons why the Government wants to expand supply.

                        2 marks                                          2 marks
                     Good application                                  Good analysis
    2
           Strong use of Figures 1, 2 and/or 3   Developed chain of reasoning explaining why higher
           to support the argument.              supply is desirable.

                          1 mark                                           1 mark
                    Limited application                               Limited analysis
    1
           Use of the data is limited with few   A chain of reasoning exists, but points are under-
           direct references.                    developed.

                         0 marks                                           0 marks
    0
           No valid application.                 No valid analysis.


Indicative content

AO2
Figure 1 shows a historically high price-to-earnings ratio of over 8, well above long-run norms.
Figure 2 shows completions persistently below the 300,000 target – a supply gap of 60-100,000
homes per year.
Figure 3 shows ownership rates among 25-34 year olds have almost halved since 1991.

AO3
Higher supply would place downward pressure on prices and bring affordability closer to historical
norms.
Increasing supply supports home ownership among younger households, addressing inter-
generational inequality (Figure 3).
A larger housing stock supports labour mobility: workers can move to take higher-productivity jobs,
supporting UK productivity and output.
The construction of new homes generates employment, tax revenue and positive multiplier effects
through the supply chain.
Meeting housing demand reduces political pressure on the Government and reduces dependence
on expensive private-rental accommodation.
Higher supply could reduce reliance on housing benefit (which costs the Exchequer around £20
billion a year) by lowering rents.
Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                 (A520U20-1)
                                                   7


                   How effective is the UK Government's housing strategy (Figure 8)
   1      3                                                                                             [10]
                   likely to be in improving housing affordability?

                    3 marks                    3 marks                           3-4 marks
              Excellent application        Excellent analysis                Excellent evaluation

           linking Figure 8 to         clearly how the policies       ineffective. Top-band answers give
           outcomes in Figures 1, 2    would improve affordability.   a well-reasoned overall judgement.
           and 7.

                  2 marks                      2 marks                            2 marks
               Good application              Good analysis                     Good evaluation

    2
           side of the argument.       how the policies would         about the policies' weaknesses.
                                       raise supply or reduce
                                       prices.

                    1 mark                       1 mark                             1 mark
              Limited application           Limited analysis                  Limited evaluation
    1
           Use of the data is less     Chains of reasoning are        Counter-arguments present but
           effective.                  under-developed.               under-developed.

                      0 marks                   0 marks                             0 marks
    0
           No valid application.       No valid analysis.             No valid evaluation.


Indicative content

Supply-side reforms (AO3)
Planning reform should in principle allow supply to respond more quickly to price signals,
increasing completions and ultimately reducing prices. Completions are well below the 300,000
target (Figure 2).
The £11.5 billion Affordable Homes Programme directly funds genuinely affordable homes, the
tenure most needed by lower-income households.

Demand-side support (AO3)
Stamp Duty relief and the Mortgage Guarantee Scheme help first-time buyers reach the bottom of
the ladder, supporting ownership among the 25-34 age group (Figure 3).
Regional rebalancing (Freeports, Investment Zones) could spread demand more evenly, easing
pressure on London and the South East (Figures 6, 7).

Weaknesses (AO4)
Planning reform has been attempted repeatedly and runs into strong local opposition. Its effects on
completions typically take years to materialise.
Demand-side measures risk raising prices because supply is inelastic: subsidising buyers without
supply reform tends to inflate prices (Figure 1).


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                               (A520U20-1)
                                                   8


Renters' reforms may reduce landlord profitability; some exit the market, reducing rental supply
and raising rents, worsening affordability.
£11.5 billion over five years is modest relative to total UK housing investment; it will not reverse
decades of decline in social housing.
The rise in mortgage rates (Figure 5) dominates many of these measures for buyers in the short
run – monthly payments on a £250,000 mortgage have roughly doubled since 2021.
Regional rebalancing depends on where demand follows investment; employment typically
remains concentrated in the South, limiting the effect.
Overall: the strategy addresses multiple dimensions but under-scales its supply-side response and
risks inflating demand. Affordability gains are likely to be slow and uneven. Allow any other valid
points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                               (A520U20-1)
                                                    9


                   Using the data, discuss the extent to which the rise in mortgage

                   rates since 2022 (Figure 5) will affect the UK housing market.
  Band              AO2 (3 marks)                 AO3 (3 marks)                   AO4 (4 marks)

                    3 marks                         3 marks                        3-4 marks
              Excellent application             Excellent analysis             Excellent evaluation
           Figure 5 used in detail       Well-developed chains of           Well-developed counter-

           both sides of the argument.   transmission to the housing        reasoned overall
                                         market.                            judgement.

                      2 marks                       2 marks                         2 marks
                   Good application               Good analysis                  Good evaluation

           side of the argument.                                            arguments.

           superficial.                  developed.                         but under-developed.

Indicative content

AO2
Figure 5 shows mortgage rates rising from around 1.8% in 2021 to around 5.1% in 2023 – the
sharpest rise in a generation. Figure 1 shows the price-to-earnings ratio moderating from around
9.1 in 2022 to around 8.3 in 2023, consistent with some cooling.

Effects on demand (AO3)
Higher mortgage rates increase the monthly cost of servicing a loan. On a £250,000 mortgage, a
rise from 2% to 5% raises repayments by roughly £400 per month, pricing many buyers out.
Reduced affordability shifts demand leftwards. Prices should fall, bringing the price-to-earnings
ratio closer to historic norms (Figure 1).
Demand from buy-to-let investors also falls as rental yields compress against rising finance costs.
Transaction volumes fall as households delay moving – the market becomes less liquid.

Counter-arguments (AO4)
Most UK mortgages are 2- or 5-year fixed deals, so the full cost shock feeds through only gradually
as fixes expire. The transmission to prices is slower than in markets with variable-rate norms.
Supply remains tight (Figure 2 shows completions still below target), limiting the price fall. A large
stock-flow imbalance can sustain prices despite weak demand.
Renters bear much of the cost: landlords pass through higher finance costs via rents, worsening
affordability for the 19% of households who rent privately (Figure 4).


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                               (A520U20-1)
                                                  10


High rates may reduce new-build activity because development finance is more expensive,
worsening the supply problem and preventing the expected price correction.
Regional effects will vary: London and the South East (with the highest prices, Figure 7) may see
the largest falls; lower-priced regions like the North East may see little change.
Existing owners may face negative equity if prices fall sharply, but only those with high LTV ratios.
Overall: mortgage rate rises clearly dampen the housing market in the short run, but the effect on
prices is gradual because of fixed-rate insulation and limited supply. Rents are likely to rise,
worsening affordability for renters even as owners benefit from modest price falls. Allow any other
valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                              (A520U20-1)
                                                     11


                   Using the data, discuss whether falling home-ownership rates
   1      5        among younger households (Figure 3) are likely to widen UK                       [10]
                   regional inequalities.
  Band              AO2 (3 marks)                AO3 (3 marks)                  AO4 (4 marks)

                    3 marks                        3 marks                       3-4 marks
              Excellent application            Excellent analysis            Excellent evaluation
    3      Figures 3, 6 and 7 used         Well-developed chains        Well-developed counter-
           together on both sides of the   explaining the link to       arguments and a well-reasoned
           argument.                       regional inequality.         overall judgement.

                      2 marks                      2 marks                        2 marks
                   Good application              Good analysis                 Good evaluation
    2
           Data used on one side of the    Developed chains of          Developed counter-arguments.
           argument.                       reasoning.

                     1 mark                          1 mark                         1 mark
               Limited application              Limited analysis              Limited evaluation
    1
           Use of data is superficial.     Chains of reasoning under-   Counter-arguments present but
                                           developed.                   under-developed.

                       0 marks                       0 marks                        0 marks
    0
           No valid application.           No valid analysis.           No valid evaluation.


Indicative content

AO2
Figure 3 shows ownership among 25-34s falling from 67% (1991) to 39% (2023) – a dramatic shift.
Figure 7 shows London prices (£525k) more than three times the North East (£160k).
Figure 6 shows regional dwellings per 1,000 people vary from 379 in London to 477 in Scotland.

Widens inequality (AO3)
Young buyers priced out of expensive regions (London, South East) need to either accept long-
term renting or move to cheaper regions. Those staying rent, building no housing equity.
Housing equity is the largest component of household wealth. Younger generations locked out of
ownership accumulate less lifetime wealth, widening the intergenerational divide – and because
wealth correlates with region, regional inequality widens.
Renters pay landlords who are disproportionately located in or connected to already-wealthy areas,
concentrating wealth further.
High London rents consume a large share of young workers' income, reducing their ability to save
for deposits – a self-reinforcing cycle trapping them in the most expensive region.
Those who do move to cheaper regions may find fewer high-productivity jobs, accepting lower
wages and dampening their long-term earnings growth.

Limits / may narrow inequality (AO4)
Falling ownership rates are a national phenomenon (Figure 3 shows it in every age group except
65+), not specifically a regional one. Regional inequality may remain unchanged.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                (A520U20-1)
                                                  12


If buyers move from London to the North to afford homes, this redistributes demand and could
narrow regional price gaps over time (Figure 7).
Regional investment (Levelling-Up Fund, Investment Zones, Freeports) is directed to lower-income
regions, partly offsetting housing-based inequality.
Remote working reduces the wage premium of London-based employment; younger workers can
earn London wages while owning a home in a lower-cost region.
Many other factors drive regional inequality: productivity differences, infrastructure investment,
education levels, industry mix. Home ownership is only one dimension.
Social housing provision varies by region; where it is more generous, falling ownership matters
less for wellbeing.
Overall judgement: falling youth ownership interacts with existing regional price gaps to widen
lifetime wealth inequality, especially between those who can afford high-priced southern regions
and those who cannot. However, it is one channel among many – broader regional policy,
productivity and infrastructure matter more. Allow any other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                              (A520U20-1)
                                                     13


Question 2

                   Using the information provided on the UK grocery market, outline
   2      1                                                                                                    [5]
                   the characteristics of an oligopolistic industry.
  Band                          AO1                                                    AO2

                                                                                 3 marks
                                                                           Excellent application
    3
                                                               Strong use of the case to illustrate multiple
                                                               features of oligopoly.

                              2 marks                                           2 marks
                           Good knowledge                                    Good application
    2
           Comprehensive understanding of the                  Good use of the case to illustrate one feature
           characteristics of oligopoly.                       well.

                                1 mark                                            1 mark
                          Limited knowledge                                 Limited application
    1
           Understanding is superficial, incomplete or         References to the case are superficial.
           contains inaccuracies.

                               0 marks                                            0 marks
    0
           No relevant knowledge shown.                        No valid application.


Indicative content

AO1 / AO2
A few large firms dominate the market: Tesco, Sainsbury's, Asda and Morrisons (the Big Four)
account for around 65% of sales. Figures 1 shows the top six firms (Big Four plus Aldi and Lidl)
hold around 83%.
High concentration ratio: four-firm concentration is around 65%.
Significant barriers to entry: economies of scale, access to prime retail sites, established supply
chains, loyalty-card data and capital requirements.
Product differentiation through branding, store format, loyalty schemes and own-brand ranges
(Clubcard Prices, Nectar Prices, Aldi Price Match).
Interdependence of firms: price-match pledges and matching promotions show clear mutual
awareness.
Non-price competition: advertising, loyalty schemes, store innovation, convenience formats, online
delivery.
Price rigidity / kinked demand curve: prices tend to be sticky; the rise of Aldi/Lidl triggered the
unusual period of price competition.
Ability to earn abnormal profits long-run: the largest firms have consistently reported strong
margins.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                 (A520U20-1)
                                                     14

                   maximise profits.

Indicative content

AO1
Profits are maximised where MC = MR (or, with discrete data, at the highest output at which MR ≥
MC).

AO2

   Price         Qty             TR        MR (£000s per 000              TC           MC (£000s per 000
    (£)        (000s)          (£000s)           units)                 (£000s)              units)

   £6.00            0              0                 –                    20                    –

   £5.50           10             55                5.5                   38                    1.8

   £5.00           20             100               4.5                   50                    1.2

   £4.50           30             135               3.5                   66                    1.6

   £4.00           40             160               2.5                   86                    2.0

   £3.50           50             175               1.5                   112                   2.6

   £3.00           60             180               0.5                   146                   3.4

   £2.50           70             175              -0.5                   190                   4.4

   £2.00           80             160              -1.5                   248                   5.8


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                 (A520U20-1)
                                                15


Credit working:
For MR and MC both correctly calculated: AO2: 3
For MR and MC correctly calculated with minor errors: AO2: 2
For either MR or MC correctly calculated: AO2: 1

AO3
Profits are maximised at an output of 40,000 lasagnes per week (price £4.00) where MR (2.5)
exceeds MC (2.0). At 50,000 units, MC (2.6) exceeds MR (1.5), so producing the extra 10,000
units would reduce profit.
Candidates may verify using total profit: TR − TC = £160k − £86k = £74k at Q = 40, compared with
£63k at Q = 50 and £69k at Q = 30.
Allow OFR.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                            (A520U20-1)

                   Using a costs and revenue diagram, evaluate the effects on a

                                         response).           change profit.

Indicative content

AO1
Initial diagram: supermarket operating at MC = MR with AR above AC at profit-max output; shaded
rectangle shows abnormal profit. Aldi/Lidl entry shifts AR (and MR) leftwards – the Big Four lose
customers to the discounters. AC may rise marginally if retained customers demand more
promotions/loyalty rewards. New equilibrium at lower Q with reduced or zero abnormal profit
rectangle.

AO2
Aldi has expanded from 3% of the market (2010) to 10.4% (2023); Lidl from around 2% to 7.9% –
together 18.3%, largely taken from the Big Four. Aldi Price Match, Tesco Clubcard Prices and
Sainsbury's Nectar Prices illustrate the competitive response.

AO3
Entry by discounters increases the number of firms in the market and makes demand more price-
elastic. The Big Four's AR (and MR) shifts leftwards.
Supermarkets respond with price-match pledges, narrowing their mark-up over costs and reducing
the rectangle between AR and AC at the profit-max output.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                (A520U20-1)
                                                   17


Some fixed costs (marketing, loyalty schemes) rise, further reducing abnormal profit.
Abnormal profit falls; in the limit, with free entry and perfectly contestable discount alternatives,
profits approach normal.

AO4
The Big Four are differentiated from Aldi/Lidl (wider range, fresh and premium lines, online
delivery). This preserves some price-setting power for branded and premium segments, limiting the
leftward AR shift.
Investment in automation, private-label expansion and supply-chain efficiency has lowered MC and
AC, partially offsetting the profit squeeze.
Aldi/Lidl are not universal substitutes: rural areas and many town centres lack a discounter; local
market power survives.
Big Four can use loyalty-card data for price discrimination, capturing additional consumer surplus
from members – defending abnormal profits.
Cyclical factors matter: during the 2022-23 cost-of-living shock some Big Four firms reported
higher margins in selected categories.
Overall: abnormal profits have fallen meaningfully since 2010 as Aldi/Lidl gained share, but the Big
Four retain some abnormal profit through differentiation, cost-cutting, data and scale. Allow any
other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                               (A520U20-1)
                                                  18


                   Evaluate the view that loyalty-pricing schemes such as Clubcard
   2      4        Prices and Nectar Prices are harmful to consumers in the UK                       [7]
                   grocery market.
  Band                     AO1                          AO3                           AO4

                                                                                   3 marks
                                                                             Excellent evaluation
    3
                                                                         Strong evaluation with a clear
                                                                         overall judgement.

                        2 marks                      2 marks                      2 marks
                     Good knowledge                Good analysis               Good evaluation
    2
           Clear understanding of loyalty     Clear chain of reasoning   Developed counter-arguments
           pricing and consumer welfare.      on consumer harm.          on consumer benefits.

                          1 mark                       1 mark                       1 mark
                    Limited knowledge             Limited analysis            Limited evaluation
    1
           Partial understanding.             Chain of reasoning lacks   Counter-arguments present
                                              clarity.                   but undeveloped.

                         0 marks                       0 marks                      0 marks
    0
           No valid understanding.            No valid analysis.         No valid evaluation.


Indicative content

AO1
Loyalty pricing is a form of price discrimination in which the same good is offered at different prices
to different customers (members vs non-members). The CMA is investigating whether these
practices harm consumers.

Consumer harm (AO3)
Two-tier pricing extracts more consumer surplus: non-members pay effectively inflated prices, so
the reference price against which "discount" is advertised may be misleading.
Schemes create switching costs: a shopper's loyalty data, points balance, and tailored offers are
lost if they switch, reducing effective competition.
Loyalty data allows supermarkets to identify price-sensitive customers and target them selectively,
rather than compete broadly on price.
Non-member prices may be raised relative to underlying cost, so the headline "saving" does not
represent a real price cut – the CMA's investigation centres on this concern.
Complexity of promotions exploits bounded rationality: shoppers struggle to compare prices across
stores when two tiers of pricing exist.

Counter-arguments (AO4)
Members genuinely pay lower prices – by a meaningful margin. For many households this is a
welfare gain even if non-members lose out.
Loyalty schemes are free to join and widely available; the "penalty" for non-membership is
avoidable.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                              (A520U20-1)
                                                 19


The data collected enables supermarkets to reduce waste, forecast demand and tailor ranges –
productive efficiency gains that can lower prices overall.
Competition between schemes (Clubcard Prices, Nectar Prices, Aldi Price Match) drives benefits
for consumers and limits each scheme's market power.
The CMA retains the power to intervene if investigations find harm; the regulatory backstop limits
the scope for exploitation.
Overall: loyalty pricing has mixed effects. Most active shoppers benefit; non-members and the
bounded-rational face a real cost. Net welfare likely positive if the scheme rewards a majority of
customers, though regulation may be needed to ensure reference prices are genuine. Allow any
other valid points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                             (A520U20-1)
                                                        20


                   With reference to the data, discuss the extent to which the UK
   2      5                                                                                                  [11]
                   grocery market leads to a reduction of economic welfare.
  Band        AO1 (2 marks)        AO2 (2 marks)              AO3 (3 marks)               AO4 (4 marks)

                                                                 3 marks                  3-4 marks
                                                                 Excellent            Excellent evaluation
                                                                 analysis         Very well-developed counter-

                                                                                  judgement.

              2 marks               2 marks                     2 marks                     2 marks
           Good knowledge        Good application             Good analysis              Good evaluation

           understanding of     use of the case on           explanation of
           economic welfare.    both sides.                  welfare effects.

                 1 mark               1 mark                      1 mark                     1 mark
                Limited              Limited                 Limited analysis          Limited evaluation
    1          knowledge            application              Partial analysis.    Counter-arguments

           of welfare.          case.

                      0                   0                          0                           0
    0
           No knowledge.        No valid application.        No valid analysis.   No valid evaluation.


Indicative content

AO1
Economic welfare is the sum of consumer and producer surplus, adjusted for externalities. It
includes allocative and productive efficiency and dynamic (innovation) effects.

AO2
Big Four concentration around 65% (Figure 1); Aldi/Lidl combined 18.3%; CMA investigations into
loyalty pricing and supplier relations in 2023-24; supermarket profit margins at the peak of the 2022
cost-of-living crisis attracted political scrutiny.

Harm to welfare (AO3)
Oligopolistic concentration allows firms to set prices above marginal cost, generating allocative
inefficiency and a deadweight loss.
Loyalty pricing extracts additional consumer surplus from non-members (CMA investigation).
Market power over suppliers – especially small farmers – can push farm-gate prices below cost,
reducing producer welfare and long-run supply resilience.
Barriers to entry (scale, prime sites, loyalty data) restrict new competition.
Dominance of large stores may contribute to decline of independent retailers and weaker high-
street competition.

Benefits / limits to harm (AO4)


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                  (A520U20-1)
                                                  21


Competition from Aldi and Lidl has delivered substantial price cuts across the sector – UK grocery
prices rose less than most EU countries during the 2022-23 shock.
Economies of scale deliver genuine productive efficiency: UK grocery prices as a share of
disposable income are historically low.
Non-price competition has widened consumer choice dramatically: range of own-brand tiers, online
delivery, convenience formats, product innovation.
CMA intervention and the threat of it constrains exploitative behaviour.
Loyalty schemes deliver real savings for the majority of shoppers.
Consumer surplus: despite the concentration, UK grocery prices have fallen in real terms over the
long run, suggesting substantial consumer benefits.
Overall judgement: the market is concentrated and exhibits some classic oligopoly welfare losses
(deadweight loss, supplier squeeze), but the combined effect of discounter entry, scale economies
and regulatory oversight means the balance of evidence suggests the market delivers net welfare
gains to consumers while imposing costs on suppliers. Allow any other valid points.
